I consider myself someone who values peace above all else. Yet it is difficult to ignore what many perceive as a pattern of double standards in the Muslim world. Iran has repeatedly signalled interest in improving relations with its regional neighbours, but instead often finds itself politically isolated and portrayed solely as an aggressor.
At the same time, several governments in the region continue to host foreign military installations and grant the United States strategic access to their territory and airspace. From Tehran’s perspective, these facilities are not neutral they are seen as part of a security architecture that directly threatens Iranian sovereignty.
Whether one agrees with Iran’s responses or not, a consistent principle should apply, regional stability cannot be built while external military power is deeply embedded in the region. If the genuine goal is de-escalation, then reducing foreign military footprints and prioritizing intra-regional dialogue would remove many of the conditions that fuel confrontation in the first place.
A truly honest conversation requires acknowledging that security fears exist on all sides and that lasting peace will only come when policies reflect mutual respect rather than selective outrage.

WHY CAN WE NOT ACHIEVE PEACE IN OUR WORLD?

It is a question that weighs heavily on countless hearts: why is lasting peace so elusive across our globe? There is a growing call for nations to focus their attention and resources on addressing the challenges within their own borders – tackling issues that directly impact their own communities, rather than engaging in military actions or interventions in other countries.

In particular, the actions and decisions of nations like the United States, Israel, and Iran are under intense scrutiny. It is critical that these countries carefully consider the profound consequences of their actions, especially when they put innocent civilians at risk. The loss of life, displacement of families, and widespread suffering caused by conflict are devastating and far-reaching.

There is deep concern that ongoing tensions could escalate into a larger, global confrontation – potentially even leading to a third world war. This is a scenario that no one desires, as the human and economic cost would be catastrophic for all of humanity. We must all hope and work toward a future where dialogue, diplomacy, and mutual respect replace conflict and aggression, ensuring the safety and well-being of people everywhere.

Back to the 1997 Act, yes even if the bases are permanent since 1999 in Szczecin and in Poland and other places, the act addresses *substantial combat forces* . Not infrastructure.
The agreement is fully honored in that;
1. The forces are not substantial
2. The forces are not permanent.
It is the infrastructure that is permanent. In fact, the Act requires that NATO have *adequate infrastructure* in place.
The combat forces stationed there are neither substantial nor permanent as they are deployed on rotational basis. So how can you say NATO violated that? They didn't.

Don't forget that Ukraine didn't start trying to join NATO in 2008. Their push to join began in 2002. The whole time, they are rejected, then promised, then postponed - like I said earlier.
All because of NATO trying to listen to Russia's opposition. Even though the 1997 Act which Russia is a party to respects every country's sovereign right to choose, and respects NATO's open door policy.

So what exactly did NATO violate? If they had disagreements over how to interpret the Act, wouldn't they be negotiating with NATO instead of trying to rob a neighbor of its territory?
Russia is all about controlling territory. Either they do it through vassal states, or they invade them and annex like they have done to those Eastern European peoples throughout history.
They all have adverse experience with Russian aggression.

And even if Estonia and Latvia are not large countries, their combined border with Russia is still significant. And now add Finnish border and Russia would have a far bigger problem than Ukraine -if the problem was really NATO forces.

If Russia could simply just wait and negotiate about NATO in Finland without such urgent war, then it could just as much have waited and negotiated about Ukraine without launching a war given that Ukraine was not even being admitted to NATO anyways.
Just being promised, then postponed, and taking years and years. Since 2002.
In fact, NATO was never going to accept them until Russia started bullying them hard.

And what does Ukraine have against Russia? Why do they feel they need NATO in the first place? Because Russia wouldn't stop dictating their lives.
So how can any normal person support their war against Ukraine? It's nothing but a war of dominion and empire.
It has no equivalence to Israel. Israel is exactly in the same place Ukraine is. Only that it's stronger than its enemies. They try to sweep in to destroy Israel because they are Jews (their religion teaches them to subdue the Jews, and they have done in the past), but Israel knocks them out each time.
Then they try again and again.
Of course Israel's survival is very clearly threatened.
Russia's isn't.
Russia launches wars for the pride of its past imperial glory, Israel launches preemptive attacks to stop or prevent impact of wars that are coming at it anyways when they still have an advantage.
